EVENT_NARRATIVE: A violent EF-4 tornado with peak winds of 190 mph impacted southern Williamson County, IL, during the early evening hours of Friday, May 16, 2025. The tornado began near the intersection of Spillway Road and Grassy Road doing mainly tree damage. As it moved eastward, the tornado intensified as it approached I-57. The tornado strengthened to EF-3 levels, where it removed the roofs of several homes of the staff of the U.S. Penitentiary just south of the main prison complex. The tornado debarked, and reduced to stubs, numerous trees adjacent to I-57. Multiple videos of this tornado were taken around the time it crossed I-57. The tornado continued at EF-3 strength as it damaged homes along IL-37 and north of Hudgens Road. The tornado reached peak intensity after crossing Market Road and into a subdivision just east of Andrew Road and north of Quarter Horse Road. Four homes on Kyler Court and Leslie Court sustained EF-3 damage. In addition, a newer two-story home with standard construction was completely swept off its foundation at the end of Kyler Court, which supported a rating of EF-4 with peak winds of 190 mph. The wooded area just east of Kyler Court saw extreme stubbing and some debarking of trees. The tornado continued eastward, destroying or severely damaging several homes at EF-2 or EF-3 strength on Wards Mill Road. Additional homes were destroyed at EF-3 damage levels a couple miles to the east on Highway 166. After crossing Highway 166, the tornado turned to the east-southeast and began to weaken, eventually lifting near the intersection of Mauseyville Road and Tyler Bridge Road. There were no fatalities, but seven people sustained non-life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line pushed eastward overnight across the warm sector of a low pressure system in Iowa. A surging portion of the line produced wind damage in portions of Southern Illinois, including the formation of a brief tornado in eastern Union County. MLCAPE was around 1000-1500J/kg, effective bulk shear was 40-50kts, and 0-3km SRH was 200-300m2/s2, with dewpoints in the mid to upper 60s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a car in Herrin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following the lifting of a tornado just west of Mount Pleasant, straight line wind damage uprooted a tree on Woods Rd and brought down tree branches further east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was draped across the southern Illinois from the evening of the 5th through the morning of the 6th. As a result, skies were clear and winds were calm, which allowed dense fog to develop generally along and east of the Interstate 57 corridor overnight through sunrise. The fog first developed near Mount Vernon in the late evening of the 5th, and gradually spread southward through the pre-dawn hours on the 6th.

Long-lingering flooding from the major flood event in April resulted in minor flooding at Carmi to start May. River levels briefly dropped below flood stage May 3rd; however, heavy rainfall the night of May 3rd into early morning the 4th pushed water levels back into minor flood for another week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding on the Little Wabash River continued into May following the major flood crest on April 11th. River levels dropped just below flood stage on the 3rd. Flood impacts in early May near Carmi were limited to flooding of low-lying areas and a few county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage pushed through Southern Illinois near sunset, resulting in scattered thunderstorms. One of these storms produced nickel size hail in Johnston City. MLCAPE values were around 1000J/kg, DCAPE was 800-1000J/kg, and effective bulk shear had dropped to below 30kts, while dewpoints ahead of the front were in the mid to upper 60s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two reports of nickel sized hail were made in Johnston City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 500mb shortwave trough moved across the Upper Midwest with height falls across the Quad State during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The exit region of a strong (125kt) upper level jet was positioned across the area as well. Low-level winds strengthened during the afternoon to 50kts at 850mb. Following the early AM convection, ample sunshine allowed for a very unstable airmass to develop with CAPE of 3000-4000J/kg with deep layer shear of 65-70kts. Late afternoon, STP peaked at 9 near the Wabash/Ohio River confluence with effective SRH of 300-400m2/s2. Mid-level lapse rates peaked at 8-8.5C/km for much of the Quad State with significant hail parameter of 3-5 mid-afternoon and DCAPE peaking at 1200-1500J/kg. Initial storm formation was discrete with very large hail and tornadoes, with storms becoming more linear later in the evening with the approach of a cold front.||Damage reports were widespread near and south of IL-13 in Southern Illinois. An especially powerful supercell produced a violent tornado south of Marion with peak winds estimated at 190mph, the strongest in the Quad State region since the December 10, 2021 Western Kentucky tornado. The worst of the damage was between Andrew Rd and Wards Mill Rd with the EF-4 damage point a home swept off its foundation on Kyler Ct. This supercell would continue on to produce two more tornadoes (in Southeastern Saline and Southern Gallatin Counties) before crossing into Northwestern Kentucky. Large hail was commonplace with the afternoon supercells, with the largest reports being multiple baseball size hail reports in Pulaski County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated at half dollar size fell at WSIL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of March 14, 2025, numerous severe thunderstorms developed across central Missouri and tracked east. After sunset, these thunderstorms began producing tornadoes, some strong, across portions of Missouri and Illinois. In total, 13 tornadoes touched down in the CWA, making it one of the most active outbreaks on record for the St. Louis CWA. Six of these tornadoes occurred at least partially in St. Louis's Illinois counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 11:11 PM a tornado began just southwest of Hagarstown, IL in Fayette County and continued northeastward for 13.4 miles through Vandalia, IL before dissipating southwest of Sefton, IL in Fayette County around 11:23 PM. The worst damage occurred northeast of Vandalia, where several trees were uprooted. This tornado was assigned a rating of EF-1 with a maximum wind speed of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Semi-discrete convection developed over portions of southwest Illinois during the early afternoon of March 30th. These thunderstorms produced large hail as they tracked east. The largest hailstones reported in Illinois were 1.75 inches in diameter. One of these was reported in Evansville in Randolph County, and three other reports came from Marion County.

A slow-moving cold front interacted with a very moist, unstable airmass during the afternoon and evening of June 30th, 2025, resulting in a cluster of thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall along and south of I-70 late that evening. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3.5 inches occurred, which resulted in a few instances of flooding beginning on June 30th and lingering into the early morning hours of July 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over IL Route 1 between St. Francisville and Mt. Carmel, beginning at 22:59 LST on June 30th. Other side streets were also inundated by water. This flooding lingered into the early morning hours of July 1st, dissipating by 01:45 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe QLCS moved through south-central Illinois during the afternoon of July 16th. As the system passed, it produced tree damage in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree in the Godfrey area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe QLCS moved through south-central Illinois during the afternoon of July 16th. As the system passed, it produced tree damage in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree in the general location of this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection developed in south-central Illinois during the very early hours of July 17th. Although flash flooding was the primary concern overnight, one thunderstorm was able to produce wind damage in portions of Macoupin County as it passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree on Valley Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree along Route 66.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed along and south of a cold front during the afternoon of July 17th. These thunderstorms congealed and dumped 4+ inches of rain on portions of southwest Illinois over 2 hours, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flood conditions on Route 13 south of Freeburg where 4 to 5 feet of water flooded the road. One car also stalled in water on US-50 west of Trenton where both directions were flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A caller reported that 4.05 inches of rain fell in 2 hours in St. Rose. Here, 2 to 3 feet of water was reportedly covering roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms forced by a weak low-level jet moved into west-central Illinois during the early morning hours of July 19th. One thunderstorm intensified as it passed over Pittsfield, causing a tree to fall onto a home. No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked a tree onto a home in Pittsfield. The tree was about 12 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 500mb shortwave trough moved across the Upper Midwest with height falls across the Quad State during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The exit region of a strong (125kt) upper level jet was positioned across the area as well. Low-level winds strengthened during the afternoon to 50kts at 850mb. Following the early AM convection, ample sunshine allowed for a very unstable airmass to develop with CAPE of 3000-4000J/kg with deep layer shear of 65-70kts. Late afternoon, STP peaked at 9 near the Wabash/Ohio River confluence with effective SRH of 300-400m2/s2. Mid-level lapse rates peaked at 8-8.5C/km for much of the Quad State with significant hail parameter of 3-5 mid-afternoon and DCAPE peaking at 1200-1500J/kg. Initial storm formation was discrete with very large hail and tornadoes, with storms becoming more linear later in the evening with the approach of a cold front.||Damage reports were widespread near and south of IL-13 in Southern Illinois. An especially powerful supercell produced a violent tornado south of Marion with peak winds estimated at 190mph, the strongest in the Quad State region since the December 10, 2021 Western Kentucky tornado. The worst of the damage was between Andrew Rd and Wards Mill Rd with the EF-4 damage point a home swept off its foundation on Kyler Ct. This supercell would continue on to produce two more tornadoes (in Southeastern Saline and Southern Gallatin Counties) before crossing into Northwestern Kentucky. Large hail was commonplace with the afternoon supercells, with the largest reports being multiple baseball size hail reports in Pulaski County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measured to golf ball size fell near Old Hwy 13 in Murphysboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with an outflow boundary triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms in the Illinois River Valley during the morning of July 8th.  This initial band of showers tended to dissipate toward midday: however, as the atmosphere destabilized during peak heating, an uptick in convective development was noted further east along and east of I-55 by late afternoon into the evening.  With ample deep-layer moisture in place as evidenced by precipitable water values of 1.75-2.00, the storms that developed were efficient rain-producers.  In addition, atmospheric steering winds were quite weak...leading to slow storm movement.  The end result was highly variable rainfall amounts...with a few spots picking up over 3 inches in a short amount of time.  One of the hardest hit locations was Bloomington-Normal in McLean County where widespread amounts of 3 to 5 inches caused flash flooding of several area streets.  Further south, a few of the cells produced isolated wind damage across parts of Sangamon and Shelby counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown onto power lines.

In the late afternoon of June 3rd, a line of thunderstorms developed in northeast Missouri, and spread into far southeast Iowa and Illinois.  These storms rapidly became severe. The thunderstorms quickly moved northeast across southeast Iowa, west central, and northwest Illinois between 530 PM and 8 PM CDT.  This line of storms produced 8 tornadoes, along with isolated pockets of higher end wind damage from estimated winds of 80 to 100 mph. A cluster of several tornadoes moved into the the southern portions of the Quad Cities IA/IL metro area, producing EF1 damage into Moline Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 100 mph touched down on the south side of Monmouth, and move northeast through town, damaging trees, sheds, and a garage. A roof was torn off a mobile home, and roof damaged on a house. It continued northeast where it tore the roof off a shop building and damaged trees southeast of Gerlaw.

The thunderstorms quickly moved northeast across southeast Iowa, west central, and northwest Illinois between 530 PM and 8 PM CDT.  This line of storms produced 8 tornadoes, along with isolated pockets of higher end wind damage from estimated winds of 80 to 100 mph. A cluster of several tornadoes moved into the the southern portions of the Quad Cities IA/IL metro area, producing EF1 damage into Moline Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This longer lived EF1 tornado, with maximum winds of 100 mph, tracked from southwest of Coyne Center into the southern Quad Cities metro area ending near the Avenue of the Cities in Moline. This tornado did heavy tree damage near and in a mobile home park in Coyne Center, including tearing the roof off one home, smashing most the walls on another, and downing a large tree smashing one of the homes. The tornado appeared to pass just on the western side of the Quad Cities International Airport before crossing I-280. The tornado produced heavy tree damage on the north side of the Rock River, including some trees downed onto garages / sheds. The tornado would pass very close if not partially over SouthPark mall in Moline, causing damage to numerous trees in the parking lot, and damaging nearby commercial signs and windows. Windows were also reported blown out in several cars at a business. Into Moline damage from the tornado became more sporadic until it approached 53rd street where more trees were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees damaged in Ponderosa Hills subdivision, including several uprooted.  Time estimated using radar.

This line of storms produced 8 tornadoes, along with isolated pockets of higher end wind damage from estimated winds of 80 to 100 mph. A cluster of several tornadoes moved into the the southern portions of the Quad Cities IA/IL metro area, producing EF1 damage into Moline Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large swath of straight-line wind damage with winds estimated 80 to 90 MPH. Numerous trees damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms brought several rounds of torrential rainfall to portions of northeast Illinois on the 12th, especially around the interior Chicago metro area. Street flooding was reported around a number of municipalities. Nearly 500 service requests were made to the city of Chicago for water in basements. One person was also injured at Montrose Beach as lightning struck nearby.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station northeast of Pontiac recorded 4.25 of storm total rain from approximately 1 PM through 6:15 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms moved into Muscatine on the morning of July 19th. This produced heavy rain with trained spotters reports ranging from 3 to 4 inches of rain. This rain falling on already saturated ground led to flash flooding in Macomb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported flooded streets with 4 to 6 inches of water.

Strong winds on the 21st generated high waves and deadly currents on southern Lake Michigan beaches, resulting in several water rescues and a couple of fatalities. The Harrison-Dever Crib was regularly reporting wind gusts between 45 and 55 mph during the afternoon out of the southwest, with a peak gust of 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 27 year old female was boating with friends off of Grand Ave near Navy Pier on the afternoon of the 21st when she fell into the water. Friends made attempts to save her, throwing ropes, life vests, and even jumping in after her, but were unsuccessful. She was transported to a local hospital where she was pronounced deceased.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six people were boating at 63rd St Beach on the afternoon of the 21st when two of the occupants fell into the water. One managed to get to safety, while the other, a man, remained missing. Rescue efforts turned to recovery efforts in the days following, but were unable to locate the man's body.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall and widespread flooding to much of the Chicago metro area, namely the south and west suburbs and up into the city of Chicago. Several Oswego District 308 schools received water and storm damage. Flood damages were estimated at around $288 million across the metro area, most of which came from the city of Chicago which alone accounted for roughly $230 million in damages with roughly 40,000 individual structures flooded around the city. The remainder of Cook County accounted for roughly $57 million in flood damages with more than 10,000 additional structures flooded. Several instances of wind damage were also reported, mostly damage to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb had fallen onto parked cars at 89th Pl and Melvina Ave in Oak Lawn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level ridge of high pressure over the Midwest brought a period of hot and very humid weather to central Illinois in mid to late July.  High temperatures on July 23rd reached the lower 90s...while surface dewpoints in the upper 70s to around 80 degrees led to peak afternoon heat index readings in the 105-110 degree range.  A 61-year old man died of hyperthermia in Peoria during the afternoon of the 23rd while heat index values reached a maximum of 107 degrees at the Peoria Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61-year old man was found unresponsive outdoors in Peoria on July 23rd.  Temperatures at the time were in the lower 90s while heat index readings were around 107 degrees.  The man was given emergency treatment for hyperthermia, but died in the hospital a short time later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary interacting with a warm and very humid airmass triggered a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) over central Illinois during the late evening of July 25th.  Nearly continuous lightning was observed with the storm cluster along with torrential rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour.  The storms also created scattered minor wind damage from near Petersburg in Menard County northeastward to Waynesville in DeWitt County before gradually weakening later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and tree branches were blown down.

The storms were primarily focused south of the I-70 corridor where rainfall rates of 1-3 inches per hour led to isolated flash flooding in Crawford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rising water forced the closure of Route 1 north of Flat Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with an outflow boundary triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms in the Illinois River Valley during the morning of July 8th.  This initial band of showers tended to dissipate toward midday: however, as the atmosphere destabilized during peak heating, an uptick in convective development was noted further east along and east of I-55 by late afternoon into the evening.  With ample deep-layer moisture in place as evidenced by precipitable water values of 1.75-2.00, the storms that developed were efficient rain-producers.  In addition, atmospheric steering winds were quite weak...leading to slow storm movement.  The end result was highly variable rainfall amounts...with a few spots picking up over 3 inches in a short amount of time.  One of the hardest hit locations was Bloomington-Normal in McLean County where widespread amounts of 3 to 5 inches caused flash flooding of several area streets.  Further south, a few of the cells produced isolated wind damage across parts of Sangamon and Shelby counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a foot of water was flowing across the road at the intersection of East College Avenue and Airport Road near the Four Seasons II gym.

Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of northern Illinois during the late evening of July 25th into the early morning of July 26th producing heavy rain and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos showed a well developed funnel cloud extending two thirds of the way to the ground near Serena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with an outflow boundary triggered scattered showers and thunderstorms in the Illinois River Valley during the morning of July 8th.  This initial band of showers tended to dissipate toward midday: however, as the atmosphere destabilized during peak heating, an uptick in convective development was noted further east along and east of I-55 by late afternoon into the evening.  With ample deep-layer moisture in place as evidenced by precipitable water values of 1.75-2.00, the storms that developed were efficient rain-producers.  In addition, atmospheric steering winds were quite weak...leading to slow storm movement.  The end result was highly variable rainfall amounts...with a few spots picking up over 3 inches in a short amount of time.  One of the hardest hit locations was Bloomington-Normal in McLean County where widespread amounts of 3 to 5 inches caused flash flooding of several area streets.  Further south, a few of the cells produced isolated wind damage across parts of Sangamon and Shelby counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across the road near the intersection of College Avenue and Linden Street in Normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst with estimated peak winds near 80 mph started near Lisle High School at the intersection of Ohio Court and Short Streets and continued east along Riedy Road, ending near Interstate 355. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of July 20th, an MCS rode into south-central Illinois. Though the system as a whole kept pushing southeast during the evening, back-building and additional development along its northwestern edge caused it to rain constantly over Fayette County for hours. Between about 7:45 pm CDT and midnight, central Fayette County received 8.7 inches of rain of a total 13.5 inches. This resulted in flash flooding in the form of impassable roadways and stranded vehicles. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for central Fayette County during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple towns and municipalities in northern Fayette County were affected by flash flooding according to emergency management, broadcast media, law enforcement/fire, and members of the public. In Wrights Corner, multiple roads had at least several inches of water covering them, with the intersection of N 2800 and County Road 2 having 3 to 6 feet of water on it. In Ramsey, rain rates of greater than 3 inches per hour caused 3 to 5 inches of water over the road at 8th Street and Fayette Street. The Ramsey Blacktop outside of town was also impassable due to water over the road. Near St. Elmo, a car was stalled in knee-deep water and the fire department was called to rescue the stranded individual. In Avena, US-40 was impassable due to flooding, and a vehicle and fire truck became stuck in around 2 feet of water. In Brownstown, a family's car became stuck in flood water, but they were able to walk out on their own. Near Shafter, a vehicle became stuck in flood water at County Road 2200 and N 200. E 1500 was flooded out in Saint James. Near Vandalia, a vehicle rolled over in 4 feet of flood water on I-70. Overall, this area received up to 13.5 inches of rain with 7.39 inches falling in 4.25 hours.

Between about 7:45 pm CDT and midnight, central Fayette County received 8.7 inches of rain of a total 13.5 inches. This resulted in flash flooding in the form of impassable roadways and stranded vehicles. A Flash Flood Emergency was issued for central Fayette County during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a culvert to wash out on Polk Avenue. This destroyed Polk Avenue north of Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 18th, a few rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall to much of northern Illinois resulting in flash flooding. One particularly strong storm also produced damaging winds in DeKalb County. Initial reports estimate $8.8 million in flood damages around northern and northeast Illinois, and around $2.5 million in the city of Chicago alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS WFO Chicago measured 1.51 of rain in the one hour between 5 and 6 PM CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received via mPing of street flooding north of 55th St west of Fairview Ave in Downers Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 18th, a few rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall to much of northern Illinois resulting in flash flooding. One particularly strong storm also produced damaging winds in DeKalb County. Initial reports estimate $8.8 million in flood damages around northern and northeast Illinois, and around $2.5 million in the city of Chicago alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding impacted portions of far northeast Winnebago County, specifically in the vicinity of Rockton. Street flooding was reported near Rockton Rd and Dorr Rd with floodwater reaching into yards. Street flooding was also reported via mPing along I-39 south of McCurry Rd. A private weather station on the east side of Rockton recorded 4.5 of rainfall. Two others nearby recorded closer to 3.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of northern Illinois during the afternoon of July 10th producing large hail, wind damage and heavy rain.  Localized flash flooding occurred around Rockford.  Additional thunderstorms moved across central portions of Illinois during the late evening of July 10th into the early morning of July 11th producing isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain fell across portions of Rockford, Cherry Valley and Loves Park in a very short time during the late afternoon of July 10th.  Several vehicles were stranded in flood waters a few feet deep in multiple locations and street flooding occurred in many areas.  Video showed waist deep water on Argus Drive between Buckley Drive and Sundae Drive where at least two cars and a city transit bus were stuck in flood waters.  Six passengers were on the bus when it stopped because of the flood waters and there were no injuries reported.  A photo showed 2 to 3 feet of standing water on Golf Hill Road at Putter Place.  A photo showed water at least one foot deep on Alpine Road at Charles Street.  A photo showed flooding along East State Street.  Flood waters entered at least one home and one apartment building.  One motorist commented that the flooding developed very quickly as they were driving.  At least 17 cars were stalled in flood waters, most just past the bottom of the doors but 2 cars had flood waters over the hoods.  Hurricane Harbor, an amusement park, was flooded and was closed for 10 days.  Rainfall of 2.62 inches in one hour was reported two miles east southeast of Machesney Park at 1626 CST.||Additional thunderstorms moved across some of the same areas during the early evening with additional flooding reported in Loves Park where flood waters entered the garage of an industrial building on Beacon Street.||Storm total rainfall amounts included 6.50 inches one mile east of Loves Park; 5.05 inches two miles north northwest of Machesney Park; 4.41 inches one mile northwest of Machesney Park; 3.93 inches south southwest of Rockton and 3.78 inches two miles south southeast of Loves Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of July 26th-July 27th, moisture convergence along a low-level jet spurred convection in eastern Missouri. This convection developed in an environment primed for efficient and heavy rainfall. Thunderstorms trained over Calhoun County, causing roads to flood and culverts to wash out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calhoun County sheriff reported at least 5 trees across roads around Hamburg. The closed roads in the county include Route 100, Michael Hamburg Road, Treshman Hollow Road, Irish Hollow Road, Gresham Hollow Road, East Panther Creek Road, and Indian Creek Hollow Road. Several of these roads were closed due to pavement or culverts being washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe QLCS moved through south-central Illinois during the afternoon of July 16th. As the system passed, it produced tree damage in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree at the Ramsey substation. The storm then moved southeast where it snapped tree limbs of up to 4 inches in diameter in St. Elmo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 16th, a series of severe thunderstorms produced widespread damaging winds and instances of severe hail around the western and northwestern Chicago metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home along Hemingway Ct in Plainfield knocking out multiple electrical systems. Time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 7:50 PM CST on the 20th, Evanston FD received a report of a teenager that had gone under the water while swimming at Lighthouse Beach. The 17-year-old boy was recovered and transported to a nearby hospital. He was later announced deceased. A high swim risk was in effect advertising life threatening swimming conditions with waves three to five feet high.
During the afternoon and evening of the 16th, a series of severe thunderstorms produced widespread damaging winds and instances of severe hail around the western and northwestern Chicago metro area.

A series of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall and widespread flooding to much of the Chicago metro area, namely the south and west suburbs and up into the city of Chicago. Several Oswego District 308 schools received water and storm damage. Flood damages were estimated at around $288 million across the metro area, most of which came from the city of Chicago which alone accounted for roughly $230 million in damages with roughly 40,000 individual structures flooded around the city. The remainder of Cook County accounted for roughly $57 million in flood damages with more than 10,000 additional structures flooded. Several instances of wind damage were also reported, mostly damage to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station in Oswego recorded 6.02 of 24-hour rainfall, 5.42 of which fell between 2 and 6 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered strong to severe thunderstorms in the Illinois River Valley during the evening of August 18th.  A few of the cells produced 60-70mph wind gusts and minor wind damage, as well as prolific lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 mph wind gusts on the north side of Peoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure over the middle Mississippi River Valley resulted in temperatures surging to the mid-to-upper 90s with dew points in the mid 70s, yielding heat indices around 105-110 mainly from the 16th-19th in Southern Illinois with some locally higher values. A few sites reached heat indices high enough for duration based heat criteria starting on the 14th-15th, mainly near the Ohio/Mississippi River confluence. Southeastern Illinois had more frequent dew points in the lower 70s, limiting heat advisory criteria duration. A cold front the night of the 19th-20th brought an end to the heat. The highest observed heat index was 119 on the 18th at the Crab Orchard RAWS site.

A jagged line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon across Southeast Missouri and Southern Illinois, dipping southward through mid afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall led to the failure of a drain pipe coupling at Oswego East High School. The failure took place on the third floor of the school causing water damage to spread to the lowest three levels. Hard ceilings had to be redone, carpets were extracted and cleaned, drywall was repaired, and lower insulation was replaced. Damages totaled between $200,000 and $250,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms tracked across the area early Sunday August 10th, bringing very heavy rain, frequent lightning, and some localized gusty winds. Rainfall rates over 2/hr on top of saturated ground caused flash flooding with water over roadways and creeks overflowing. Late afternoon and evening storms brought more heavy rain and three brief tornadoes in Lee county in Iowa and Clark county in Missouri. No damage was reported or seen with these tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in town, time estimated  from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and closed low reached the Quad State from the northwest during the morning of the 4th. The arrival of the shortwave preceded peak heating by several hours, limiting convective potential. Shear was very weak and instability limited. Slow-moving storms did produce torrential rainfall, with flooding issues reported in Hamilton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was stuck in water on IL-14 at Ten Mile Rd around the time rainfall rates peaked at around 0.75'' in 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak southwest flow aloft, ahead of an approaching upper-level trough, induced low-level moisture return across southern Illinois through the day. By late afternoon, surface-based CAPE was around 3000 J/kg and downdraft CAPE was 800-1000 J/kg. With weak flow aloft, the shear was also weak. In the early evening an isolated storm produced a significant downburst as it moved east northeast across Grayville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees and large tree limbs in Grayville. Some of the downed trees took down powerlines. A brick wall of a business was partially knocked down.

The remainder of Cook County accounted for roughly $57 million in flood damages with more than 10,000 additional structures flooded. Several instances of wind damage were also reported, mostly damage to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Oswego High School, prolonged intense rainfall overwhelmed the capacity of a storm drainage system. Up to two feet of water flooded a courtyard near the school. Water flowed from the courtyard into the school via a set of double doors and reached depths of 8 to 10 inches near the entrance to the building. Water proceeded to flow into hallways, athletic facilities, and the gymnasium. The damage required the gym floor to be replaced. Damages amounted to over $300,000, with the gym floor alone costing around $250,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 18th, a few rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall to much of northern Illinois resulting in flash flooding. One particularly strong storm also produced damaging winds in DeKalb County. Initial reports estimate $8.8 million in flood damages around northern and northeast Illinois, and around $2.5 million in the city of Chicago alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of torrential rainfall brought flash flooding to parts of southeast Lee County. A video shared to social media showed a house in the Paw Paw area surrounded by floodwater with significant flooding in the yard and in nearby fields. Paw Paw Rd was flooded approximately one mile north of Paw Paw. In Compton, water was seen flowing over roads and parking lots. A private weather station in Paw Paw recorded 7.02 of storm total rain, 5.03 of which fell between 3:15 and 5:15 CST. Another private weather station near Dunn Airport northwest of Paw Paw recorded 4.67 of storm total rainfall. The flooding is estimated to have resulted in $5,000 worth of damages, mostly to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad high pressure over the middle Mississippi River Valley resulted in temperatures surging to the mid-to-upper 90s with dew points in the mid 70s, yielding heat indices around 105-110 mainly from the 16th-19th in Southern Illinois with some locally higher values. A few sites reached heat indices high enough for duration based heat criteria starting on the 14th-15th, mainly near the Ohio/Mississippi River confluence. Southeastern Illinois had more frequent dew points in the lower 70s, limiting heat advisory criteria duration. A cold front the night of the 19th-20th brought an end to the heat. The highest observed heat index was 119 on the 18th at the Crab Orchard RAWS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough of low pressure triggered a few clusters of thunderstorms across central and southeast Illinois during the afternoon and evening of August 4th.  One of the most prominent clusters dropped 2.44 of rain in a short amount of time at the Lawrenceville Airport and caused flash flooding along Illinois Route 1 on the north side of Lawrenceville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported up to 3 feet deep along Illinois Route 1 on the north side of Lawrenceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough interacting with a seasonably warm and moist airmass triggered a couple bands of thunderstorms across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of August 12th.  One of the strongest cells produced excessive rainfall rates and caused isolated flash flooding in Decatur in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An underpass was flooded and a car stalled in the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms formed in Southern Illinois during the afternoon of the 13th in the vicinity of a cold front as a weak shortwave trough was draped across the area. One of these showers produced a cold air funnel near Tamaroa. The environment had decent low-level moisture with drier mid-levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media and emergency management reported a cold air funnel with a shower near Tamaroa.

For the third month in a row, much of the area experienced below normal rainfall, with deficits of 1-3 inches in most areas north of I-70. Temperatures were also well above normal during the first half of the month. These conditions led to persistence or worsening of the ongoing severe drought over the course of the month. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal, some communities implemented burn bans, and isolated, voluntary water restrictions began. Areas near and south of I-70 saw near normal precipitation, which led to some improvement of the drought conditions during the second half of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a two-month rainfall deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. Well below normal rainfall and above normal temperatures led to a worsening of the drought during October, with Extreme Drought (D3) conditions noted between October 7th and October 21st. Severe Drought (D2) then persisted from October 21st through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a two-month rainfall deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. The cooperative observer located three miles south of Champaign measured 1.53 inches of rainfall during October, which was 1.82 inches below normal. Temperatures were also well above normal during the first half of the month. Drought conditions worsened to Extreme Drought (D3) by October 14th, and remained extreme through the end of the month. A burn ban that began in September was continued through October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm conditions were in place across the Midwest on Friday, October 3rd, with temperatures in the upper 80s as of early afternoon. Scattered, slow-moving showers and thunderstorms developed, one of which produced a brief landspout tornado in Vermilion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a public video of a landspout tornado viewed from the southeast edge of Fairmount looking southeast towards Jamaica. The witness reported that the watched the landspout for a few minutes. The video shows the landspout over open fields. Since no damage occurred, a damage intensity rating could not be assigned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms developed and moved all the way across southern Illinois in the early morning. The storms formed just ahead of a strong, sharp 500mb trough in an area of strong upper divergence associated with a small 120kt 300mb jet. Most unstable CAPE was only around 250J/kg, but mid-level lapse rates were 6.5-7 C/km and low-level lapse rates were 6-6.5 C/km. Right near the trough line, the shear was negligible. The storms began to produce damaging winds across eastern portions of Perry County and then solidified into a persistent bow echo over Franklin County. It produced damaging winds as it moved northeast through McLeansboro and into White county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped off and fell onto a mobile home on Blue Bird Lane on the south side of Tamaroa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions from September continued into October, with portions of Southern Illinois from Murphysboro to Fairfield remaining in D2 (Severe Drought) status until mid-month. Drought onset began with a very dry August and first half of September, with a rainy late September. The first half of October was dry for this area, as heavy rain passed by to the south on October 6th. Heavy rain on October 18th, with 1-2 inches of rain in the Severe Drought region, finally improved drought conditions to D1 status in the following update. Harvest season was ongoing, resulting in limited additional agricultural impacts for Southern Illinois.

A large wildfire occurred near Stelle in Ford County and rapidly spread northeast, crossing into a small part of Iroquois County.  The fire is suspected to have been started by hot farm equipment harvesting crops on a dry and windy day.  The fire is estimated to have burned nearly 650 acres.  A few farm outbuildings were destroyed by the fire.  Over 40 fire departments responded to the wildfire with ash from the fire being deposited as far north as east central Will County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started in a crop field southeast of Stelle near 3500N Road between 1800E Road and 100E Road.  The fire rapidly spread northeast crossing 3600N and 3700N Roads and then entered far northeast Iroquois County.  Several hundred acres were burned, some with unharvested crops.  A few farm outbuildings were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass continued to destabilize across central Illinois during the afternoon of December 28th as high temperatures reached record values in the upper 60s to around 70 degrees and dewpoints hovered in the lower to middle 60s.  Thanks to the high degree of instability and shear, a line of thunderstorms rapidly formed along an advancing cold front near the I-55 corridor by early afternoon.  The storms then pushed eastward, producing 3 tornadoes and scattered damaging winds eastward to the Indiana border.  The most significant tornado was rated EF-2 and caused considerable property damage just south of Decatur in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down about 3 miles east-southeast of Saybrook at 3:24 PM CST.  The tornado destroyed a large outbuilding, throwing the debris to the east-northeast.  It then quickly dissipated before reaching the Ford County line at 3:25 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through central Illinois on December 13th, introducing windy and sharply colder conditions.  Thanks to clearing skies and northwesterly winds flowing over a fresh 3 to 8-inch snow cover, air temperatures dropped below zero by the early morning of December 14th.  Given the continued gusty northwesterly winds, wind-chill values bottomed out in the -20 to -35 degree range north of I-70 and east of I-55.  Despite the dangerously cold conditions, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -25 to -35 occurred across Vermilion County during the morning of December 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance brought a period of light snow to central and southeast Illinois during the afternoon and evening of December 1st.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches...with a few pockets approaching 4 inches from Macomb eastward to Lincoln and near Effingham.  Snow-covered roadways led to a fatal car crash in northern Knox County between Oneida and Wataga.  No other traffic accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fresh snowfall of 1 to 3 inches led to snow-covered and hazardous roadways across Knox County during the afternoon and evening of December 2nd.  A man was killed when his vehicle collided with a semi truck on US Route 34 between Oneida and Wataga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with an advancing Arctic cold front brought a period of heavy snow to central Illinois on December 13th.  Despite limited deep-layer moisture, strong synoptic lift and high snow-to-liquid ratios of 15:1 to 20:1 led to enhanced snowfall rates and an efficient accumulation.  Storm total snowfall was 3 to 6 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with as much as 6 to 8 inches occurring along a Canton...to Bloomington...to Danville line.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents and road closures were reported.  A seven-vehicle accident on southbound I-57 in Coles County resulted in the death of a man and the injury of his wife.  Meanwhile a separate multi-vehicle crash on I-55 near the Clear Lake exit in Springfield claimed the life of a teenaged girl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers measured 6 to 8 inches of snow across Woodford County...including 7.5 inches in Eureka and 6.0 inches in Metamora.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fifth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits generally between 0.5 and 1.5 inches for the month of December. Temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but fell below normal during December. While precipitation was below normal for the month, disturbances in late November and throughout the month of December did provide enough precipitation to lead to localized improvements in the drought, particularly south of I-72 and in areas to the west and north of Peoria. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought persisted through the month, but did not worsen. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a four-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. However, multiple disturbances during the second half of November produced sufficient precipitation to improve the drought conditions to Moderate Drought (D1) by December 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass continued to destabilize across central Illinois during the afternoon of December 28th as high temperatures reached record values in the upper 60s to around 70 degrees and dewpoints hovered in the lower to middle 60s.  Thanks to the high degree of instability and shear, a line of thunderstorms rapidly formed along an advancing cold front near the I-55 corridor by early afternoon.  The storms then pushed eastward, producing 3 tornadoes and scattered damaging winds eastward to the Indiana border.  The most significant tornado was rated EF-2 and caused considerable property damage just south of Decatur in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado crossed into far western Macon County from Christian County about 1 mile west-northwest of Blue Mound at 3:00 PM CST.  It tracked east-northeastward and remained weak until it crossed US Highway 54 and reached Elwin.  Between Elwin and Turpin, several homes sustained roof damage and numerous large trees were snapped.  The most significant tornado damage occurred along East Skyline Drive as the roofs of 8 homes were stripped off, a garage was completely destroyed and tossed to the next block, and very large trees were knocked down.  As the tornado continued east-northeastward, it caused more roof and tree damage between South Baltimore Avenue and South Mount Zion Road.  The tornado then weakened, but continued to snap tree branches and cause shingle damage to a few homes as it passed through the Long Creek area eastward to the Macon/Piatt County line.  It then tracked into western Piatt County just north of the intersection of Ocean Trail and NE County Line Road at 3:30 PM CST.

Several trees at this property were damaged including one pine tree whose top half was snapped off and tossed nearly 100 yards to the east.  At an adjacent property, a barn was shifted off of its foundation and had a portion of its roof peeled off.||As the tornado reached the Iroquois River, it tore the roof off of a garage and peeled roofing material off of a house at a property next to the river. Extensive tree damage was noted at this property and just to the east along the river, where multiple trees were uprooted and dozens of trees lost limbs.  The tornado weakened a bit east of the Iroquois River, but continued to cause tree damage as it approached and crossed N 1800 East Road.  A residence along N 1900 East Road and two additional barns along N 2000 East Road were damaged by the tornado.  The tornado lifted southwest of St. Anne, shortly after it tipped over an irrigation pivot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front swept through central Illinois on December 13th, introducing windy and sharply colder conditions.  Thanks to clearing skies and northwesterly winds flowing over a fresh 3 to 8-inch snow cover, air temperatures dropped below zero by the early morning of December 14th.  Given the continued gusty northwesterly winds, wind-chill values bottomed out in the -20 to -35 degree range north of I-70 and east of I-55.  Despite the dangerously cold conditions, no cold-related injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind-chill values of -25 to -35 occurred across McLean County during the morning of December 14th. The coldest hourly wind-chill reading observed at the Bloomington Airport (KBMI) was -25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of central Illinois during the late morning of December 28th producing localized wind damage.  Additional severe thunderstorms moved across parts of central Illinois during the afternoon producing additional wind damage and six tornadoes.  Heavy rain also caused localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado with peak winds of 85 mph touched down just west of N 1300 East Road near Langan Creek, northeast of Clifton producing minor tree damage. Little to no damage was observed as the tornado moved across open farm fields on either side of Route 45.  The tornado peeled roof shingles off a house and damaged multiple farm outbuildings along N 1500 East Road, just north of E 3200 North Road.  A barn was blown off of its foundation, while a garage collapsed entirely.  The tornado lifted before reaching the Iroquois River as no damage was observed to the tree line along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fifth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits generally between 0.5 and 1.5 inches for the month of December. Temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but fell below normal during December. While precipitation was below normal for the month, disturbances in late November and throughout the month of December did provide enough precipitation to lead to localized improvements in the drought, particularly south of I-72 and in areas to the west and north of Peoria. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought persisted through the month, but did not worsen. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a four-month precipitation deficit, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought was ongoing at the start of the month. Despite above normal snowfall during December, precipitation was still below normal, and the continued below normal precipitation led to the Severe (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3) persisting through the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a four-month precipitation deficit, Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) Drought was ongoing at the start of the month. However, precipitation that occurred late in the month of November led to the removal of Extreme Drought by December 2nd. The cooperative observer in Decatur reported 2.70 inches of precipitation during December, which was 0.30 inches above normal. However, this was not enough precipitation to improve the drought, and Severe Drought (D2) persisted through the month. Water levels on Lake Decatur remained 2.5 to 3 feet below normal, and voluntary stage 1 water rationing continued in the city of Decatur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with an advancing Arctic cold front brought a period of heavy snow to central Illinois on December 13th.  Despite limited deep-layer moisture, strong synoptic lift and high snow-to-liquid ratios of 15:1 to 20:1 led to enhanced snowfall rates and an efficient accumulation.  Storm total snowfall was 3 to 6 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with as much as 6 to 8 inches occurring along a Canton...to Bloomington...to Danville line.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents and road closures were reported.  A seven-vehicle accident on southbound I-57 in Coles County resulted in the death of a man and the injury of his wife.  Meanwhile a separate multi-vehicle crash on I-55 near the Clear Lake exit in Springfield claimed the life of a teenaged girl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers measured 3 to 5 inches of snow across Cass County...including 4.5 inches in Beardstown.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents occurred.

Winds were frequently gusting between 40 mph and 50 mph with higher peak gusts, including a gust to 60 mph at the Sugar Grove Airport.

An upper-level disturbance interacting with an advancing Arctic cold front brought a period of heavy snow to central Illinois on December 13th.  Despite limited deep-layer moisture, strong synoptic lift and high snow-to-liquid ratios of 15:1 to 20:1 led to enhanced snowfall rates and an efficient accumulation.  Storm total snowfall was 3 to 6 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with as much as 6 to 8 inches occurring along a Canton...to Bloomington...to Danville line.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents and road closures were reported.  A seven-vehicle accident on southbound I-57 in Coles County resulted in the death of a man and the injury of his wife.  Meanwhile a separate multi-vehicle crash on I-55 near the Clear Lake exit in Springfield claimed the life of a teenaged girl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather observers measured 5 to 6 inches of snow across Douglas County...including 5.5 inches in Tuscola.  Due to snow-covered and slick roadways, numerous traffic accidents occurred.

Severe thunderstorms moved across portions of central Illinois during the late morning of December 28th producing localized wind damage.  Additional severe thunderstorms moved across parts of central Illinois during the afternoon producing additional wind damage and six tornadoes.  Heavy rain also caused localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado with estimated peak winds of 80 mph touched down just southeast of the intersection of N 400 East Road and E 2900 North Road, west of Clifton.  Three utility poles were snapped as the tornado moved northeast across E 2900 North Road.  Roofing material was peeled off of a house and trees were damaged at the intersection of N 500 East Road and E 2900 North Road.  As the tornado crossed N 600 East Road and E 2950 North Road, two farm outbuildings had portions of their roofs ripped off at the same property. Additional tree damage was noted. The tornado lifted before reaching N 700 East Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado with peak winds of 90 mph touched down just south of 4H Park Road on the northwest side of Pontiac and immediately moved northeast across the Motorola and Vermillion Estates Subdivisions.  The tornado caused roofing and siding damage to a number of houses, apartments and manufactured homes.  Two houses had large portions of roofing blown off, leave the attics exposed.  The tornado crossed Route 23 and produced additional siding and fascia damage to a few manufactured homes in the Redwood mobile home park before ending north of E 1830 North Road.  The tornado also damaged several trees along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fifth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits generally between 0.5 and 1.5 inches for the month of December. Temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but fell below normal during December. While precipitation was below normal for the month, disturbances in late November and throughout the month of December did provide enough precipitation to lead to localized improvements in the drought, particularly south of I-72 and in areas to the west and north of Peoria. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought persisted through the month, but did not worsen. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a four-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. Despite above normal snowfall during December, precipitation was still below normal, and the continued below normal precipitation led to the Severe Drought (D2) persisting through the month.

As a 1000mb low over southeast Iowa and its associated cold front began pushing eastward, a cluster of severe thunderstorms developed west of the Illinois River across Fulton County, then tracked eastward across portions of Tazewell and Woodford counties before eventually weakening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The airmass continued to destabilize across central Illinois during the afternoon of December 28th as high temperatures reached record values in the upper 60s to around 70 degrees and dewpoints hovered in the lower to middle 60s.  Thanks to the high degree of instability and shear, a line of thunderstorms rapidly formed along an advancing cold front near the I-55 corridor by early afternoon.  The storms then pushed eastward, producing 3 tornadoes and scattered damaging winds eastward to the Indiana border.  The most significant tornado was rated EF-2 and caused considerable property damage just south of Decatur in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fifth consecutive month, much of the region experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits generally between 0.5 and 1.5 inches for the month of December. Temperatures were above normal for much of autumn, but fell below normal during December. While precipitation was below normal for the month, disturbances in late November and throughout the month of December did provide enough precipitation to lead to localized improvements in the drought, particularly south of I-72 and in areas to the west and north of Peoria. Elsewhere, the ongoing drought persisted through the month, but did not worsen. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a four-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. While Severe Drought (D2) persisted for much of the month, above normal snowfall occurred during December and a frontal system brought 1 to 3 inches of rain to the area late in the month. The official observation for Peoria was 2.00 inches of precipitation, which was near normal. Drought conditions improved to Moderate Drought (D1) by December 30th.

The airmass continued to destabilize across central Illinois during the afternoon of December 28th as high temperatures reached record values in the upper 60s to around 70 degrees and dewpoints hovered in the lower to middle 60s.  Thanks to the high degree of instability and shear, a line of thunderstorms rapidly formed along an advancing cold front near the I-55 corridor by early afternoon.  The storms then pushed eastward, producing 3 tornadoes and scattered damaging winds eastward to the Indiana border.  The most significant tornado was rated EF-2 and caused considerable property damage just south of Decatur in Macon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a dairy barn was destroyed and the top of a tree was snapped.

The highest measured gust of 58mph occurred at Capital Airport in Springfield.  No wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58mph non-thunderstorm wind gust was observed at Capital Airport (KSPI) in Springfield at 10:15am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter storm tracked across the region Friday night through late Saturday night.  Widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches. This will likely go down as one of the highest snowfall events for any year for the month of November in northwest Illinois. Numerous accidents including a few jackknifed semis on I-80 and I-74 and several road closures occurred with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer at Gladstone Lock and Dam reported 10.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure slowly crept northward towards the Quad State region, ridging remained over the middle Mississippi River Valley. A slow-moving MCS moved through the northeastern half of the Quad State midday to near sunset. CAPE rose to 1000-2000J/kg in northern portions of Southern Illinois with effective bulk shear around 30kts. Extremely high moisture content with precipitable water values of 2.25in resulted in torrential rainfall rates along and near the I-64 corridor. Several areas reported flash flooding, especially northern Wayne County which had radar estimated totals of up to half a foot in northwestern portions of the county and sandbags deployed in Cisne. Flash flooding was also reported in Hamilton, Franklin, and Williamson counties. Albion reported 3.12in of rainfall within four hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on Timber Trail and West Grand Avenue. Radar estimated hourly rainfall amounts for this area peaked at around 1.25. The lack of much subsequent rainfall limited the flood duration.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in Cisne and in northwestern Wayne County. Multiple roads were impacted and sandbagging was deployed. Cisne received a radar estimated 3-4 inches of rain, while northwestern portions of Wayne County approached half a foot of rain for the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was positioned west to east across the Quad State during the early afternoon with convective activity increasing with daytime heating. Scattered convection primarily occurred south of the boundary mid to late afternoon. MLCAPE reached 2000J/kg but effective bulk shear was very limited. Precipitable water values over 2 allowed for torrential rainfall rates. Training storm occurrence was limited, leaving flash flooding south of Harrisburg as the one flood report out of Southern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a barn on Mermet Rd, which resulted in a fire that fully engulfed the barn and burned it down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary separating an unseasonably warm airmass with temperatures in the lower 70s south of the I-64 corridor from much cooler readings in the upper 40s to around 50 degrees across central Illinois served as the focus for scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of November 18th.  The storms were primarily focused south of I-72 and east of US-51 and produced hail ranging from the size of peas (0.25 inches in diameter) to as large as tennis balls (2.5 inches in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo indicated the hailstones were spiky and oblong-shaped.

Low pressure tracking from western Kansas during the evening of November 28th to southern Lake Michigan by the morning of November 30th brought widespread heavy snowfall to much of central Illinois.  The snow began during the pre-dawn hours of November 29th, then became moderate to heavy at times during the morning and afternoon of the 29th as the low approached.  Despite the wet nature of the snow, strong southeasterly winds gusting 30-40mph created considerable blowing and drifting.  As the low tracked toward the southern Great Lakes, it pulled a warm front through the region during the evening...leading to temperatures rising into the middle to upper 30s and the lingering very light precipitation to change to drizzle or sprinkles.  Once the low tracked further away, colder air returned and caused the slush and water on roads to re-freeze overnight into the morning of November 30th, creating slick and hazardous driving conditions.  Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 10 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with a few locations from eastern Sangamon County into southern Christian County picking up as much as 11 to 12 inches.  Daily snowfall records were established at Peoria (5.2 inches), Springfield (8.9 inches), and Lincoln (7.8 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous weather observers across the northern half of Cumberland County reported 5 to 9 inches of snow...including 8.5 inches 3 miles north-northeast of Neoga.  Due to both falling snow and considerable blowing and drifting snow, travel became extremely hazardous...leading to numerous accidents on area roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth month in a row, much of the area experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 in most areas during the month of November. Temperatures were near or slightly above normal. These conditions led to a persistence or worsening of the ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought over the course of the month. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal, including near record low streamflows on eastern portions of the Sangamon River (upstream of Lake Decatur). In response to these drought conditions, some communities implemented burn bans or voluntary water rationing. Some agricultural impacts were noted, including a wreath selling business that had to cancel sales due to impacts on the evergreen crop. ||Precipitation was well below normal across all of central and southeast Illinois, with most sites reporting a deficit between 1 and 2 inches. Drought conditions persisted or worsened. Monthly average streamflows across most of central and southeast Illinois were classified below normal, including near record low flow on portions of the eastern Sangamon River. Temperatures were near to slightly above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a three-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. Below normal precipitation persisted during November, with the cooperative observer in Galesburg reporting 1.65 of precipitation which was 0.95 below normal. Temperatures were also slightly above normal during the month. These conditions led to a persistence of the Severe Drought (D2) through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth month in a row, much of the area experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 in most areas during the month of November. Temperatures were near or slightly above normal. These conditions led to a persistence or worsening of the ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought over the course of the month. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal, including near record low streamflows on eastern portions of the Sangamon River (upstream of Lake Decatur). In response to these drought conditions, some communities implemented burn bans or voluntary water rationing. Some agricultural impacts were noted, including a wreath selling business that had to cancel sales due to impacts on the evergreen crop. ||Precipitation was well below normal across all of central and southeast Illinois, with most sites reporting a deficit between 1 and 2 inches. Drought conditions persisted or worsened. Monthly average streamflows across most of central and southeast Illinois were classified below normal, including near record low flow on portions of the eastern Sangamon River. Temperatures were near to slightly above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a three-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. Below normal precipitation persisted during November, with the official observation for Lincoln measuring 1.93 of precipitation which was 0.95 below normal. Temperatures were also slightly above normal during the month. These conditions led to a persistence of the Severe Drought (D2) through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking from western Kansas during the evening of November 28th to southern Lake Michigan by the morning of November 30th brought widespread heavy snowfall to much of central Illinois.  The snow began during the pre-dawn hours of November 29th, then became moderate to heavy at times during the morning and afternoon of the 29th as the low approached.  Despite the wet nature of the snow, strong southeasterly winds gusting 30-40mph created considerable blowing and drifting.  As the low tracked toward the southern Great Lakes, it pulled a warm front through the region during the evening...leading to temperatures rising into the middle to upper 30s and the lingering very light precipitation to change to drizzle or sprinkles.  Once the low tracked further away, colder air returned and caused the slush and water on roads to re-freeze overnight into the morning of November 30th, creating slick and hazardous driving conditions.  Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 10 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with a few locations from eastern Sangamon County into southern Christian County picking up as much as 11 to 12 inches.  Daily snowfall records were established at Peoria (5.2 inches), Springfield (8.9 inches), and Lincoln (7.8 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous weather observers measured 6 to 12 inches of snow across Christian County...including 12.3 inches in Pana and 10.7 inches 3 miles west of Edinburg.  Due to both falling snow and considerable blowing and drifting snow, travel became extremely hazardous...leading to numerous accidents on area roadways.

An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 22nd through the 30th in Southern Illinois, following a more off and on heat the third week of July for northern portions of Southern Illinois. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 on the 28th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||For the entirety of the mid-late month heatwave, Carbondale, IL experienced 21 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point, their record longest. Carbondale observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 100 degrees from July 22-30, which is tied for their 3rd longest streak on record (records going back to 1996). The highest heat index in Southern Illinois for late July was 121 at the Crab Orchard RAWS site in Williamson County on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme heat continued from the third week of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended heat wave across the Quad State region occurred from mid to late July. On the synoptic scale, at 500mb, a 594dam ridge was present across the Southeast U.S., with the ridge building northward to include the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes by July 22nd-23rd. Convection coverage became more isolated the 22nd-25th as the ridge strengthened over the Ohio Valley. The ridge relaxed slightly on the 26th-27th, before the worst of the heat arrived on the 28th-29th. A cold front moving through on the 31st allowed for a much cooler and drier airmass to move across the region.||High temperatures in the low to mid 90s combined with very high humidity (dew points in the mid 70s to near 80) produced heat index values routinely in the 102 to 112 range from July 22nd through the 30th in Southern Illinois, following a more off and on heat the third week of July for northern portions of Southern Illinois. Slight decreases in heat indices occurred on the 26th-27th as more clouds were around, followed by the most oppressive heat with widespread heat indices of 110-120 on the 28th. The heat wave continued through the 30th, then a cold front moving south through the region brought relief to close out the month. Heat Advisories or Extreme Heat Warnings were in effect for portions of the Quad State for 15 consecutive days.||For the entirety of the mid-late month heatwave, Carbondale, IL experienced 21 consecutive days with a 75 degree dew point, their record longest. Carbondale observed 9 consecutive days reaching a heat index of at least 100 degrees from July 22-30, which is tied for their 3rd longest streak on record (records going back to 1996). The highest heat index in Southern Illinois for late July was 121 at the Crab Orchard RAWS site in Williamson County on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was positioned on the northern edge of the Quad State region throughout the 14th. Late afternoon into the evening, showers and thunderstorms developed as a shortwave moved through with a funnel cloud visible to the south of Mount Carmel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was visible with a shower south of Mount Carmel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the fourth month in a row, much of the area experienced below normal precipitation, with deficits of 1 to 2 in most areas during the month of November. Temperatures were near or slightly above normal. These conditions led to a persistence or worsening of the ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought over the course of the month. Streamflows and lake levels remained below normal, including near record low streamflows on eastern portions of the Sangamon River (upstream of Lake Decatur). In response to these drought conditions, some communities implemented burn bans or voluntary water rationing. Some agricultural impacts were noted, including a wreath selling business that had to cancel sales due to impacts on the evergreen crop. ||Precipitation was well below normal across all of central and southeast Illinois, with most sites reporting a deficit between 1 and 2 inches. Drought conditions persisted or worsened. Monthly average streamflows across most of central and southeast Illinois were classified below normal, including near record low flow on portions of the eastern Sangamon River. Temperatures were near to slightly above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a three-month precipitation deficit, Severe Drought (D2) was ongoing at the start of the month. Continued below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures led to the Severe Drought (D2) persisting through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking from western Kansas during the evening of November 28th to southern Lake Michigan by the morning of November 30th brought widespread heavy snowfall to much of central Illinois.  The snow began during the pre-dawn hours of November 29th, then became moderate to heavy at times during the morning and afternoon of the 29th as the low approached.  Despite the wet nature of the snow, strong southeasterly winds gusting 30-40mph created considerable blowing and drifting.  As the low tracked toward the southern Great Lakes, it pulled a warm front through the region during the evening...leading to temperatures rising into the middle to upper 30s and the lingering very light precipitation to change to drizzle or sprinkles.  Once the low tracked further away, colder air returned and caused the slush and water on roads to re-freeze overnight into the morning of November 30th, creating slick and hazardous driving conditions.  Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 10 inches everywhere north of the I-70 corridor...with a few locations from eastern Sangamon County into southern Christian County picking up as much as 11 to 12 inches.  Daily snowfall records were established at Peoria (5.2 inches), Springfield (8.9 inches), and Lincoln (7.8 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous weather observers measured 5 to 10 inches of snow across Knox County...including 8.0 inches in Knoxville.  Due to both falling snow and considerable blowing and drifting snow, travel became extremely hazardous...leading to numerous accidents on area roadways.

Snow began in the early morning hours of Saturday, November 29 and quickly became heavy. Snowfall rates reached 1 inch per hour or more for a time. Snow gradually transitioned to rain as the temperature warmed up after sunrise, but several inches had already blanketed the region by then. This was a heavy, wet snowfall that quickly coated roads and led to hazardous travel conditions primarily Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 8 to 10 inches of snow fell primarily during the morning hours. The snowfall rate reached 1 inch per hour at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front stalled out as it moved towards the northern edge of the Quad State on the 17th. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, tracking eastward, including a broken line of storms moving through the Evansville Tri-State area mid-afternoon. An outflow boundary from a decayed MCS to the north helped initiate some of the storm development. MLCAPE was a very strong 4000J/kg but bulk shear was limited. Precipitable water values were a very high 2.2 inches. Despite an active weather day, the lack of shear kept severe weather potential limited, and the lack of training with the heavy rain kept flooding from becoming widespread. The lone report out of Southern Illinois was of ponding of roads in Benton from heavy rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates of around an inch in a 45 minute span resulted in large amounts of water ponding on roadways in Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on parts of northeastern Illinois as a compact system of low pressure moved east of Lake Michigan on the 9th and into the morning of the 10th. The most snow fell in northeastern Lake County as well as north-central Iroquois into eastern Kankakee counties. Reports of 6 to 10 inches came out of Lake County. In Iroquois and Kankakee counties, as many as 9 to 12 inches fell in the course of about six hours. The city of Chicago was gapped by the higher accumulations with O'Hare Airport having received 1.7 inches in total.
